frame_label: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
generated_comment: Bro honestly I’m not judging, but girls also have to be practical yaar. If she’s travelling alone late night, partying and all, then at least be careful about where, with whom, what time — zamaana theek nahi hai and men won’t suddenly change. Later something happens and everyone asks the same thing only: why was she alone that late?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames women’s safety as their personal responsibility and treats male violence as inevitable rather than accountable. It encodes protective/practical advice, restriction around late-night movement, and post-assault victim-questioning ('why was she alone that late?').
original_frame: The Character Double Standard: Women with Male Connections are Loose, Men with Female Connections are Desirable
persona_text: You are Arjun. Age 27. Single. A man in Bengaluru. You are on a third date with a woman named Kavya who mentioned casually that most of her close friends are male Ñ she finds it easier, less drama, she has always been like this. You are back home texting your best friend about the date. You are telling him what Kavya said about her friend group and what you think it means and whether you see this as a red flag and what kind of woman has mostly male friends and whether you would pursue something serious with her.
